transcript.transcript: Today is October 31, 2006. I am interviewing Ralph McAdams at his home in Boise, Idaho. I lived on the north end of Boise Avenue, which was one mile from the park. And There were some other youngsters my age that lived in that neighborhood in the block on Boise Avenue that was residential. South of there in those days was farmland for, oh, about a half a mile, and then it was residential again. But we grew up with Our playground was kind of a circle of a mile and a half around that neighborhood. And that included things like the Howard Platt Gardens at the Union Pacific Railroad Depot, the airport, which was where the university is now. And the people that operated the hangars on the south side of the airport were friendly to kids and would let us look at the airplanes and so forth. And just on the other side of the airport, that is the university grounds, was Julia Davis Park and the river. And it was a real magnet to those of us that considered it part of our neighborhood. And we truly did, because we could just cross on the Capitol Boulevard bridge, which had been built in 1935, there was sidewalk all the way from our neighborhood. There wasn't sidewalk in our neighborhood, but as soon as you got out of it, you were on the sidewalk and you could walk all the way to the river and cross the bridge on the sidewalk and then you were in the park. And on the south side of the park is where you entered was the band shale And the playground with all of the swings, real first class merry-go-round, you know, a hand-pushed merry-go-round. Hand-pushed? With horses you sat on? No. Just a circle. Yeah, a circle. But somebody had to push it around. But it was a nice one. It was really first class. And slides, you know, the big metal slides. And there was one of them that was a great high thing to just scare the heck out of a little kid. And then there was a stream. The playground was kind of between a stream. There was a river, of course, on the south side, and then on the north side there was a little bit of a stream for several years that you could look into, you didn't get into it. And then finally they blocked that off And it just came to the street, the road on the east side of the playground. Then just North from the end of the playground, that east end of the playground, was the zoo with animals in it that were always on display. The southernmost display was a bear and sometimes more than one bear in a rustic looking cage. It was fixed so that it looked like maybe something a bear would want to live in. So it was all very handy and very interesting and a lot of fun to go to. And then in the wintertime, On the east end of the park, almost to Broadway, was a big pond. And I guess the weather was colder in those days. Every winter That froze over, and it was very nice for skating, and it was used a lot. There was a floodlight for night skating. And generally, people would bring, including my father, who was a skater, would bring wood from home and things and build a fire. And the skating in the part of the winter when it was frozen over was so good that we'd skate every day or every night. And there were, oh gosh, commonly 25 people around there. But there was always somebody. And some years the thing would freeze almost solid and all kinds of crazy things would happen, including high school kids that tried to drive their cars on the ice. But it was a really great place to skate. I enjoyed that a lot. My dad happened to be a seasoned skater and could do jumps and tricks and so forth, and taught me how to do the ones that I could learn. I was never as good as he. And there were two neighborhoods of us kids that lived either on Boise Avenue or on what is now, I guess, called College Boulevard or University Boulevard. In those days, It was called a whitey. And we'd kind of gang up together and go over there. And it was, I don't know, it was kind of considered part of our neighborhood, certainly by us, and I think by some others, because the kids from the East End didn't come there. Then in the summertime, one of the areas of interest was the southern connection to the park was the Boise River. And in those days, the Boise River didn't have Anderson Ranch Dam and Lucky Peak Dam, and didn't have a water allotment for fish. So in the summertime, when the canals were taking the river water, which wasn't as plentiful because there wasn't all that storage that there is now, the river would get quite low. And it was easy to get to the park by wading the river for a kid, a preteen kid. That was the adventure of the week was to take off your shoes and socks and wait across the river. So we did that often, hoping that we wouldn't get caught either by one of the cops who knew all of us or our folks. And the other thing was when the river was very low and the bottom of the river was kind of made up of big, round wash boulders kind of thing that you could walk on. But there were also lots of fish in there. And the main fish that we would see were suckers and carp. And these were five pounders. They were big fish. And occasionally, we'd decide we needed to harvest one or two of those, and we did that. Well, the only tools or ammunition we had were willow limbs. And we'd tear up a willow tree at the edge of the river, the river that was lined with those and make a weapon out of it or a tool and spear ourselves a fish. Well, having speared the fish, then you got to try to figure out what are you going to do with it. Can't take it home in that condition. And some of us knew that the Chinese people that operated a Chinese garden over on the west side of the foot of the Depot Hill, Capitol Boulevard Depot Hill, would buy them. So I don't think I ever took one over there to sell it, but I kind of have a mixed memory of that. But we did take some over there, and they did buy them. They give you two bits for one of them, and they knew how to cook them. These were nice, fresh fish. This wasn't a trout, but it was a keeper. And it was a lot of food for somebody. So we did that. I know we took some over to that spot that I just mentioned on the west side. Plus, there was a Chinese garden on Boise Avenue, up by the site of the old Ridenbaugh estate, only on the north side of Boise Avenue. And I think somebody took one up there, too. I didn't. But that Chinaman was very friendly to kids and a nice guy. The Chinamen were always very nice to youngsters. They weren't very forward. But if you did talk to them, why they would talk to you. And they grew and sold vegetables? They would use the fish just for their personal and domestic use? Yeah, the fish was for themselves. But they did, yes, they grew vegetables. And there was one of them, or a representative of them, another Chinaman, that had an old Dodge truck with an open back, covered with the sides open on it, what later came to be called bird cages, would come around on a weekly route and sell vegetables to your mother or whoever happened to be there. And they were great vegetables. It was a real service to the community. Did you ever go to band concerts? Yes. Tell me more about that. Well, I went to a lot of them, but I can't tell you a lot about them. The area in front of the band shell was different than it is now. It was flat instead of banked up like it is. And you just sat there in the grass or brought a chair. I never did bring a chair, but until later years. And listened to whatever it was. They didn't have an electric sound system like they do now. But it was very pleasant. I can't remember specifically. Was this the city band? Yes. Did they have uniforms? No, I don't think so. But they could have. There was a band that was somewhat sponsored by Boise City. And that went way back to the turn of the century. Also the 25th Army band used that as a venue and still does. Once or twice a year, they put on a concert there. But I don't remember ever listening to them there when I was a youngster. I've listened to them a number of times at that location in recent years, recent decades. The Army Band? Uh-huh. But your memory of when you were a youngster going, Was it like in the afternoon? Probably. Yeah, probably. I don't know. Parallel to this, I was involved starting about 1937 in Music Week. And so it's hard to keep it separated. And that was pretty intense. Some of it was pretty intense. Did any of that happen at Joey Davis Pipe? I don't think it did until modern times. It does now. Boise Music Week puts on a Saturday show there every year and has for probably 25, 30 years. They still do that. And that's at the band show. In the band show. The band show is intensely used and has been all these years. And nowadays, on the first week in May, I don't think Music Week can get it for a total day. They get it for 2 3rds of a day. Well, it works out. It's used as a base for people that are abusing the park, even for something else than sound. That happens. In fact, that's one of the things we ran into for this upcoming next year's music week. We've got a conflict, but we can work with it. There was also a refreshment stand in the park in those days. It was pretty close to the bandstand. It was right on the edge of that perimeter road that goes on the south side of the park, and a little bit east of the bandstand. It was a good-sized thing, and they served just about anything you want, I think. I don't remember anything but drinks and milkshakes that I ever got. But like I say, it was a substantial building. It's now a house over on Colley's Boulevard. They moved it decades ago and turned it into a house. So what I'm saying is it was a substantial building to start out with. All this caterwauling about the Ten Commandments and so forth, you know, that monument was on the southwest corner of the park for decades. And nobody ever looked at it. Nobody knew it was there, including I forgot it where it was. I forgot it was there. And when all the hullabaloo started over, I had a heck of a time. I had to go down there and find it. And I served 12 years on the city council. I should have known where it was. And was often liaison to PARC. I was very involved in all that. But that was such an insignificant part of the community that nobody even knew where it was, including people that worked there. What about Big Mike? Well, Big Mike was particularly interesting to me. It was a neglected city treasure. It was given to the city by Union Pacific Railroad. And its appearance was good, but it's always needed a lot of maintenance. It needs a lot of restoration. And it should be in a location that is very accessible where it is, but people don't see it. And gosh, it's a wonderful asset. And this idea to move it from the park out to the track at the railroad depot. It seems like a good thing. But it got a lot of exposure over the years where it was. And there was also an aircraft there on a pedestal. And there's still one over at Nampa. It was a jet and it was close. It was on the same parcel of ground there. What about the paddle boats? Did you ever use those? Oh yeah, everybody did. They were great. You kind of had a choice. You could either pump the pedals and just go flapping along, or you could pull a boat along which got it going a lot better. Q. Pull it? A. Well, no, use a pole. Q. Oh, pole it. A. Yeah, pole it. Q. Okay. A. Yeah. Q. Okay. A. Yeah, we did that. I forget what it cost, but it was a modest amount. What about the merry-go-round and the amusement park? Oh there was a concessionaire came in there, I don't know who that was, it was in there for, I don't know, probably two decades maybe that long. Did a good business. But that wasn't there when you were a boy? No, no. It was there while I was on the city council. You were later on to the park, but do you remember anything special about J.A. Davis that was occurring? You were on the city council from about what year? 74 to 86 or something like that. 74 to 86. Uh-huh. We had a terrible time with muggings. There were smash and grab deals done on people's cars. People from the university, ever since the Bob Gibb Friendship Bridge was installed, it's been quite a temptation to park your car in the park if you have business over at the university. And I know I was a volunteer many times with Idaho Public Television, and I used to do that. Well, it's stupid to do that, because if it's a dark night, your car is just a sitting duck. And a smart robber knows that most cars have these automatic trunk openers so all you got to do is break one window and you have access to everything in the car and the people that own it never think of that. So they've thrown their purse in the trunk or something. Damn. Yeah, yeah. But that's always been a problem. Uh-huh. So there was a lot of... And muggings. Yes, some. And how in Boise's... Yes, some. And in the times when Boise's sex habits were an item, Julia Davis Park always got included in that to some extent. Vander Hill Is that like the Fall of 55, the Boys of Boise? Schmottlach I don't recall anything about the Boys of Boise there, but in more recent years I think. Vander Hill Like raids or… Schmottlach Yeah, or just people being accosted. Exposing? Yeah, that sort of thing. I don't think that Julia Davis Park was ever any worse than any other place in town, but because it's a park it seemed to have a little notoriety. Certainly, Again, the time I spent on the City Council, Julia Davis Park was never a problem. It's a big park and it costs plenty of money to maintain it and update it and so forth. But a lot of those things that need to be done, like some of the things that were done where the playground was, the wading pool and so forth, my Kiwanis Club built that. We did a lot of the cleaning of the river bank. You know, the river bank was originally rip-wrapped with old car bodies, refrigerators. By the park? Oh, by everybody. Sure. That was an accepted way to do. Well, it finally got to where it wasn't an accepted thing to do, and we had to get all that stuff out of there. And a lot of it was done by volunteers And a few other people with wreckers or something. So your Kiwanis Club did that as well as made the wading pool? We made the wading pool. Plus there's a big picnic shelter there in the area where we furnished that. And we repainted it and re-roofed it several times since then. And we still see it as our responsibility. I hate this. I almost can't paint this house because I hate it so bad, but I've painted that thing. And it's used a lot. It's booked. You can't use it unless you book it through headquarters. And Is that the one that's over by where the playground is now? Or the one that's in the old playground? So that would be in the west side by the band show. Yeah, we built that. Bought it, built it, and have maintained it. Poured a lot of cash into it, but that's what service clubs are for. We've done other work around there. I can't recall just precisely what right this minute, but other things have come up. And they turn to all the service clubs. And Capital City Kiwanis happens to do some of those things because we're a large club. We always have over 100 members. So it's pretty easy to field a crew. And we are effective fundraisers. We've always got money. It's a worthy something, why we've got money for it. What about the Rose Garden? Well, I personally have enjoyed the Rose Garden many times, and every year take a tour through it. And upon the occasion of the 4th of July, another Kiwanis club called the Gem State Club, serves a breakfast there by the band shell every year. And for all the years that Susan and I have been married, 25 years, We've made it a custom to go to that on the 4th of July and take some guests. And we have breakfast, and then we go walk through the rose garden, which generally is a perfect time to do it. Some years you're a little bit too late, but it's a great place to go. There's parking and people you know, and it's a nice day. It's a wonderful thing to do. I can't imagine a park in our community other than Julia Davis Park that is a great place to go for that sort of thing. That bigger crowd, that all of the needs of the crowd are served. Did you go to the art at Art in the Park? I guess in 1954, according to some of this research, the Arts and Crafts Festival began. I was probably there. You were there? I've been to it many times, as recently as this year. Yeah, I rarely missed it. Again, where else could you have it? You need space, you would like to have shade, you need the restroom facilities, parking facilities, other things to do when you're through with that, when you're there on the same site. Yeah, that's just a perfect place for that. What about when you were raising your girls? Did you ever do anything special at the park? No, I can't remember. We did go to the park. We lived just a block from Whitney School, which had a great big field. So we wanted to play some kind of ball together or something. But we just went over there. I can't remember anything that the family did particularly at Julie Davis Park. We'd go to the, you know, the three girls would go to the zoo and you'd do this and do that, and we did that. I can't remember, I guess we went to the band show. I'm sure we did that, or the Music Week show, if nothing else. Uh-huh. That's about all I remember from that. What about history at the museum? When the history comes alive. It's in the park. People dress up. And they have games, like you did just right now, in the summer. Well, I've been to it many times. This has been as recently as this year. When they've had, lots of times they'll have a few old cars down there. And I've had a car down there when it was my turn. I went there this year specifically to look at two of the cars that I knew were going to be there and ask questions of their owners because I have a car like theirs and I was thinking I was having some difficulty with some of the models. It was a Model T Ford and I thought the new style bands that I got were not as good as the old style bands and I wanted to ask them what they thought and I asked them and they told me. I got there and I said leave them alone you got what you need. And I've got some antique guns and I've been to the museum particularly when they show a few extra things.

lines.line: > The speaker describes growing up near Boise Avenue, close to various attractions such as the Howard Platt Gardens, the Union Pacific Railroad depot, and the airport. The neighborhood was a mix of residential areas and farmland, providing a rich playground for the speaker and other local children. They had access to many amenities, including the nearby Julia Davis Park, which featured a bandshell, playground, and a zoo, enhancing their childhood experience.

metadata.title: Childhood Neighborhood and Local Attractions
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes growing up near Boise Avenue, close to various attractions such as the Howard Platt Gardens, the Union Pacific Railroad depot, and the airport. The neighborhood was a mix of residential areas and farmland, providing a rich playground for the speaker and other local children. They had access to many amenities, including the nearby Julia Davis Park, which featured a bandshell, playground, and a zoo, enhancing their childhood experience.

lines.line: > The speaker recounts various activities, including fishing in the Boise River and selling the catch to local Chinese gardeners. They used makeshift tools to catch fish, which were then sold for personal consumption. This interaction highlights a small, community-based economy where local children and Chinese immigrants interacted regularly, benefiting from each other's presence in the neighborhood.

metadata.title: Seasonal Activities and Local Economy
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recounts various activities, including fishing in the Boise River and selling the catch to local Chinese gardeners. They used makeshift tools to catch fish, which were then sold for personal consumption. This interaction highlights a small, community-based economy where local children and Chinese immigrants interacted regularly, benefiting from each other's presence in the neighborhood.

lines.line: > The speaker recalls attending band concerts at the park's bandshell, describing the informal and pleasant nature of these events. They also discuss their involvement in Music Week, a significant cultural event in the community. The bandshell served as a central venue for various performances, demonstrating its importance as a community gathering spot for cultural and musical events.

metadata.title: Community Events and Music Appreciation
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recalls attending band concerts at the park's bandshell, describing the informal and pleasant nature of these events. They also discuss their involvement in Music Week, a significant cultural event in the community. The bandshell served as a central venue for various performances, demonstrating its importance as a community gathering spot for cultural and musical events.

lines.line: > During the speaker's time on the city council, Julia Davis Park faced several challenges, including muggings and thefts from cars. The speaker reflects on the security issues that arose with the park's proximity to the university and the installation of the Bob Gibb Friendship Bridge, which increased the park's accessibility and, consequently, some negative activities. Efforts were made to improve safety and maintain the park's facilities, reflecting ongoing community and governmental involvement.

metadata.title: Challenges During City Council Tenure
metadata.synopsis: During the speaker's time on the city council, Julia Davis Park faced several challenges, including muggings and thefts from cars. The speaker reflects on the security issues that arose with the park's proximity to the university and the installation of the Bob Gibb Friendship Bridge, which increased the park's accessibility and, consequently, some negative activities. Efforts were made to improve safety and maintain the park's facilities, reflecting ongoing community and governmental involvement.

lines.line: # Engagement and Contributions in Later Years

> The speaker discusses their continued engagement with Julia Davis Park through personal visits and community events. They mention attending the Art in the Park and participating in historical events where they contributed with antique displays. The speaker also highlights the role of service clubs like the Kiwanis in maintaining and enhancing park facilities, demonstrating a sustained commitment to community service and the enhancement of local amenities.

# Engagement and Contributions in Later Years

> The speaker discusses their continued engagement with Julia Davis Park through personal visits and community events. They mention attending the Art in the Park and participating in historical events where they contributed with antique displays. The speaker also highlights the role of service clubs like the Kiwanis in maintaining and enhancing park facilities, demonstrating a sustained commitment to community service and the enhancement of local amenities.
